coredata.dc:title: Ultra-fast synthesis of carbon dots using the wasted coffee residues for environmental remediation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Park, Sung Jun
Yang, Hyun Kyoung
coredata.dc:description: Currently, the coffee residues are one of waste resources which become a problem because the coffee consumption has increased. This is a worldwide problem due to the increase in disposal costs and environmental problems. Therefore, we use the wasted coffee residue which contains caffeine as starting material of carbon dot for solving disposal costs and environmental problems. The carbon dots were synthesized from coffee residues with different firing times by a facile and fast method. The emission peak of carbon dot is about 445 nm (cyan color) under 360 nm. The transmittance of the prepared carbon dot film reaches 90% which is high transparency. These results represent that carbon dot and carbon dot film can be applied for flexible display and optical application through recycling waste coffee residues.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Coffee residue
Ultra-fast
Flexible film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: synthesis of carbon dots and carbon dot film

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The scheme illustration for problem of coffee residues and recycling solution.
Schematic representation of carbon dot synthesis process using coffee residues.
(a) Photographs of coffee residue with different firing times, (b) carbon dot solutions, (c) TG-DTA curves of the coffee residues, and (d) XRD patterns of carbon dot powders.
TEM images and histogram of the carbon dots with different firing times (a) 0 h, (b) 0.5 h, (c) 1 h, (d) 1.5 h, and (e) 2 h.
XPS spectra of the prepared carbon dots with different firing times (a) 0 h, (b) 0.5 h, (c) 1 h, (d) 1.5 h, and (e) 2 h.
Absorption spectrum of the prepared carbon dots with different times 0 h, 0.5 h, 1 h, 1.5 h, 2 h.
PL spectra of the prepared carbon dots with different firing times (a) 0 h, (b) 0.5 h, (c) 1 h, (d) 1.5 h, (e) 2 h, and (f) the PL intensity against heating time for carbon dots under 360 nm.

Digital photographs of the prepared carbon dot solutions with different firing times (a) 0 h, (b) 0.5 h, (c) 1 h, (d) 1.5 h, and (e) 2 h under daylight and (a) 0 h, (b) 0.5 h, (c) 1 h, (d) 1.5 h, and (e) 2 h under UV light.

(a) UV–vis transmittance spectrum, (b) photographs of the prepared carbon dot film, (c) FE-SEM cross section of the prepared carbon dot film, carbon dot film (d) under darkroom, and (e) under UV laser.

head.ce:title: Ultra-fast synthesis of carbon dots using the wasted coffee residues for environmental remediation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Interdisciplinary Graduate Program of Artificial Inteligence on Computer, Electronic and Mechanical Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48513, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Interdisciplinary Graduate Program of Artificial Inteligence on Computer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical, Electronics and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48513, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Interdisciplinary Graduate Program of Artificial Inteligence on Computer, Electronic and Mechanical Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48513, Republic of Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Interdisciplinary Graduate Program of Artificial Inteligence on Computer

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, the coffee residues are one of waste resources which become a problem because the coffee consumption has increased. This is a worldwide problem due to the increase in disposal costs and environmental problems. Therefore, we use the wasted coffee residue which contains caffeine as starting material of carbon dot for solving disposal costs and environmental problems. The carbon dots were synthesized from coffee residues with different firing times by a facile and fast method. The emission peak of carbon dot is about 445 nm (cyan color) under 360 nm. The transmittance of the prepared carbon dot film reaches 90% which is high transparency. These results represent that carbon dot and carbon dot film can be applied for flexible display and optical application through recycling waste coffee residues.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively studied instead of rare-earth based nanoparticles [1], organic fluorescent dyes [2], and semiconductor quantum dots [3]. The inorganic semiconductor quantum dots contain heavy metals that causes harmful side effect to human [4] and environmentally hazardous [5]. The rare-earth materials have disadvantage of being very expensive because of their scarcity. In the case of fluorescent dyes, they have poor thermal and chemical stability. Therefore, inorganic semiconductor quantum dots, rare earth based nanoparticles, and organic dyes have been replaced by carbon dots which can be applied for light emitting diodes (LEDs), sensing, bio-imaging, anti-counterfeiting, solar cell, and photocatalysis [6–11].

Synthetic methods of carbon dots have been developed such as the hydrothermal [12], solvothermal, laser ablation [13], precipitation [14], microwave pyrolysis [15], ultrasonic [16], electrodeposition [17], and combustion thermal oxidation [18]. All of these synthesis methods have several problems which is complex, time consuming and harsh reaction condition. It is a cause that the increase of synthesis cost and creation of an environment harmful to humans. To settle this problem, the development to minimize synthesis process and avoid use of the hazardous materials or equipment has been researched.

Generally, carbon dots were synthesized from the precursors of conventional chemicals such as citric acid, carbohydrates, and materials based on carbon [19]. Besides, carbon dots synthesized by using the waste resources have been researched as starting materials such as paper [9], onion peel, banana stem, and plants [20–23]. Recently, the coffee residues which are one of waste resources have become a problem because the coffee consumption has increased for each year as shown in Fig. 1 . It is caused by increasing the amount of coffee residues to be treated. Therefore, we use the coffee residues from a cafe as starting material of carbon dot. As known to all, the main component of coffee residue is caffeine which serve as carbon source.

Herein, we report a green, and facile synthesis of carbon dots using the wasted coffee residues with different firing times (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5 and 2 h) and without any complex and harsh conditions. The structure of carbon dots was investigated by the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The particle size of carbon dots was observed by the transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM). The optical properties were investigated by UV–Visible spectrometer and photoluminescence spectrometer. The carbon dot film using the polyvinyl alcohol powder and carbon dots was fabricated for fluorescent film. The characterization of carbon dot film was investigated by using UV–visible and scanning electron microscope (SEM).

Waste coffee residues as carbon source were obtained by franchise café. The polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) powders were purchased from JUNSEI Co. The ethanol used in this experiment was purchased from DUKSAN Co.

ce:section.ce:section-title: synthesis of carbon dots and carbon dot film
ce:section.ce:para: Appropriate amounts of waste coffee residues were placed into an alumina crucible and fired at 400 °C on magnetic stirrer for 0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 h. The obtained series of firing coffee residue were uniformly ground. We can observe that the color of wasted coffee residues was changed from brown to black when increasing the firing time as shown in Fig. 2 . It means that the black waste coffee residues are more carbonized than the brown coffee residues. The obtained carbonized powders were filtrated with using ethanol and membrane filter. Then, we can obtain the carbon dot solutions.

1.5 g of PVA (MW = 1500 g/mol) powders was dissolved into 20 mL of deionized water under stirring using a magnetic stirrer at 90 °C for 1 h and then cooled down to room temperature. Subsequently, the prepared carbon dot solutions were poured into the prepared PVA solution. And then, the mixtures were stirred at room temperature for 1 h to obtain homogeneous solution. 4 mL of mixtures dropped into a petri dish and dried at 70 °C for 24 h. Finally, flexible film embedded carbon dots with different firing times (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5 and 2 h) was obtained.

Thermogravimetric (TG) and differential thermal analyses (DTA) of waste coffee residue were measured using a thermal analysis system with temperature from 30 to 700 °C in air with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The structure of carbon dots was analyzed with the X-ray diffraction (XRD) recording on X’PERT PRO X-ray diffractometer with a CuKα = 1.5406 Å at 40 kV of beam voltage and 30 mA of beam current. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the prepared carbon dots was performed on a PHI 5000 VersaProbe spectrometer using a monochromatic Al Kα radiation source. The morphology images of the prepared carbon dots were acquired on a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HITACHI LTD, H-7500). UV–Vis spectra of the prepared carbon dots have been recorded on a (V-670, JASCO). The luminescence properties were measured at room temperature by using a luminescence spectrophotometer (Photon Technology International (PTI)) with a 60 W Xe-arc lamp.

Fig. 3 (a), (b) show the photograph image of burned coffee residues and carbon dot solutions with different firing times (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 h). When the firing time of coffee residues increases, the color of coffee residues is changed from brown to black due to carbonization. The particle size of coffee residues with different firing times (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 h) is about (a) 15, (b) 18, (c) 20, (d) 20, and (e) 18 μm as shown in Fig. S1. It means that the firing time does not affect the particle size of carbon dots. In the case of carbon dot solutions, the color is similar regardless of firing time. TG-DTA curves were measured to analyze and select the optimal carbonizing temperature of coffee residue depending on the temperature from room temperature to 700 °C as shown in Fig. 3(c). TG curve represents the slight decrease at 25–300 °C which is related to the dehydration and evaporation of residual water and dust. From 300 to 400 °C, it can be found that much weight loss occurred by carbonization compared with DTA curve. Therefore, we decide the sintering temperature at 400 °C for carbonization. The mass loss of coffee residue around 400 °C is about 62%. XRD patterns of the prepared carbon dots with different firing times (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5 and 2 h) as shown in Fig. 3(d). The XRD patterns of carbon dots with 0 and 0.5 h are relatively low crystallinity. The XRD patterns of the prepared carbon dots represent a broad peak at 2θ = 20.7° (d = 0.45 nm), which indicates the formation of the (002) [11].

The size and morphology of the prepared carbon dots were investigated by TEM. Fig. 4 (a–e) shows the TEM images of the prepared carbon dots with different firing times (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 h). The prepared carbon dots are uniform shaped with an average particle size of (a) ∼7 nm, (b) ∼7 nm, (c) ∼6 nm, (d) ∼6 nm, and (e) ∼6 nm. It means that the firing time does not affect the particle size of carbon dots.

To confirm the element and chemical bonding of the carbon dots, the XPS spectra of the carbon dots are investigated in Fig. 5 . The carbon dots consist of two elements, that is C and O, which correspond to C 1s (282.5 eV) and O 1s (531.3 eV). The elemental analysis result of carbon dots reveals atomic ratios of C and O. The contents of C are 53.25% (0 h), 57.3% (0.5 h), 69.2% (1 h), 72.5% (1.5 h), and 74.9% (2 h). The contents of O are 46.75% (0 h), 42.7% (0.5 h), 30.8% (1 h), 27.5% (1.5 h), and 25.1% (2 h). It means that the contents of carbon increases when increasing firing time. The high resolution XPS of C 1s is composed of three peaks at 282.4 eV, 284 eV, and 286.8 eV which correspond to C–C (sp2 carbon), C–O (sp3 carbon), and CO (carbonyl group), as shown in Fig. S2. The O 1s band has two component peaks at 530.2 eV and 531.3 eV which correspond to CC and C–O–C, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 6 , the UV–Vis spectra of the carbon dots with different firing times (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5 and 2 h) show two absorption peaks. The first peak at 228 nm corresponds to the aromatic π-π* transition of CC bond and second peak at 281 nm is attributed to graphene oxide [24]. When the firing time of coffee residue increase, the intensity of absorption peak at 281 nm gradually decreases.

Fig. 7 shows the excitation dependent emission (320–440 nm) of the prepared carbon dots with different firing times (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 h). The emission wavelength also shifts according to the excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength is 360 nm, the fluorescence intensity is strong. The maximum emission wavelength is about 445 nm (cyan color). It was confirmed that the fluorescence intensity decreased as the carbonization time increased as shown in Fig. 7(f). The quantum yield (QY) was calculated using the following equation [25]:

QY = Q
Y
S T
×
(
K X
/
K
R E
)
*
(
η X
/
η
R E
)
2
, where
Q
Y
S T
is the quantum yield of the rhodamine B dye as a reference,
K X
and
K
R E
are the linear slope from the plot of the integrated photoluminescence intensity versus absorption,
η X
and
η
R E
are the refractive index. From this equation, the prepared carbon dot is obtained about 11%. Fig. 8 represents the photographs of carbon dot solution (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 h) under UV light. As shown in Fig. 8, it can be seen that the fluorescence decreases as the carbonization time increases.

Fig. 9 (a) represents the transmittance of the prepared carbon dot film embedded with carbon dots (firing time 0 h). The transmittance of the prepared carbon dot film reaches 90% in the wavelength range from 400 to 700 nm. It means that the carbon dot film has good transparency. To verify the thickness of carbon dot film, the cross section SEM image was measured as shown in Fig. 9(b and c). The thickness of carbon dot film is about 20 μm. Fig. 9(d) shows the photographs of the UV laser (365 nm) and carbon dot film under darkroom. Fig. 9(e) shows the photographs of the carbon dot film under UV laser (365 nm) which emits cyan color.

Cyan-emitting carbon dots with using coffee residues were synthesized by a facile and fast method. The coffee residues become good carbon source because it consists of the caffeine. The color of coffee residues is changed from brown to black when increasing firing time due to carbonization. The prepared carbon dots are uniform shaped with an average particle size about 6–7 nm. The PL spectra of the carbon dots represent excitation-dependent emission and strong cyan emission at 460 nm under UV excitation of 365 nm. The PL intensity of the carbon dots decreases with increasing firing time of coffee residues. The carbon dot film with using PVA powder shows flexibility and transparency. These results show that the prepared carbon dots and carbon dot film can be applied for flexible display and optical device through recycling waste coffee residues.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea(NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT; Ministry of Science and ICT) (2020R1A2C400219311).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cap.2022.01.001.
